parallels.story_headline: Iran escalates strikes across Persian Gulf as US-Israel conflict deepens and NATO credibility questioned
parallels.summary: The Tanker War was a sustained phase of the broader Iran-Iraq War in which both belligerents systematically attacked commercial shipping in the Persian Gulf to strangle each other's oil revenues and coerce third parties. Iraq opened the campaign in earnest in March 1984 by striking Iran's main export terminal at Kharg Island; Iran retaliated by targeting tankers servicing Gulf states that backed Iraq. Over the course of the conflict Iraq carried out 283 vessel attacks and Iran 168, collectively damaging more than 30 million tons of cargo and killing over 100 sailors. Shipping insurance premiums surged, global oil markets were repeatedly rattled, and the Strait of Hormuz hovered as a constant flashpoint. The United States intervened from 1986 onward, reflagging Kuwaiti tankers as American vessels and mounting Operation Earnest Will, the largest naval convoy operation since World War II. Direct US-Iranian naval clashes followed, culminating in Operation Praying Mantis in April 1988.
parallels.parallel_analysis: The current situation mirrors the Tanker War in its core dynamic: Iran using the Persian Gulf as a theatre of asymmetric pressure on a stronger adversary. The critical difference is the scale of direct US involvement. The historical record suggests sustained military pressure eventually degraded Iran's willingness to absorb costs, but the process took years.

parallels.story_headline: NASA's Artemis II launches on historic crewed journey around the moon
parallels.summary: Apollo 8 launched on December 21, 1968, as the first crewed mission to travel beyond Earth orbit and reach the Moon. Frank Borman, Jim Lovell, and William Anders became the first humans to achieve lunar orbit, witnessing an Earthrise. Their Christmas Eve broadcast was watched by 25% of the world's population. Apollo 8's success led directly to Apollo 11's landing in July 1969.
parallels.parallel_analysis: Artemis II occupies structurally the same position as Apollo 8: a crewed flyby mission validating hardware and deep-space navigation before a landing attempt. The historical record suggests the gap between flyby and landing can be remarkably short once the foundational mission succeeds.
